quote: I've actually had my house and I have a tiny house, and I had it evaluated years ago. And it basically came down to, like, there's would wouldn't be that much saving, but I don't know if things have changed.
stewart_read: This is a pre-emptive version of the home_disqualified objection — Stacy is telling Randy she has already been told solar won't work well for her. The schema's canonical response is 'Do you know why it was disqualified?' Randy never asks it. He pivots to address confirmation without acknowledging the disclosure. Strategic read: Randy may have been planning to let the specialist handle this. Reactive read: Randy didn't register it as an objection requiring a response. The reactive read holds — there is no evidence of a later setup move.
alternative_hypothesis: Randy deliberately deferred the prior-evaluation reframe to the specialist, knowing the closer would have the design data to address it concretely.
coaching_implication: Show Randy the rebuttals.home_disqualified entry. The question 'Do you know why it was disqualified?' costs nothing and surfaces whether the prior evaluation was a shading issue, a system-size issue, or a pricing issue — all of which the closer needs to know. The manager should ask Randy: 'What did you do with that line?' If he didn't notice it, that's the coaching gap.

quote: I think the city of Denver offers some financial assistance to get solar panels, so I'm considering that as well.
stewart_read: Stacy is actively considering a competing program — city-subsidized solar — and Randy does not acknowledge it at all. This is a competitive alternative that will be live in the closer's conversation. The schema's comparison_framing strategy ('it's always best to get a couple looks') applies here, but Randy never deploys it. The closer will encounter this without knowing it was raised.
alternative_hypothesis: Randy may have assessed the city program as a non-threat and chose not to elevate it. No evidence supports this — he simply moved on.
coaching_implication: The manager should ask Randy: 'Did you hear Stacy mention the Denver program?' If not, this is an active listening gap. If yes, the coaching is on why he didn't use comparison_framing to position Ion's program alongside it. The closer needs to know this program is on Stacy's radar.

quote: I'm struggling with that right now, and that's one of the reasons why I wanna save on electricity is the financial strain is difficult.
stewart_read: Stacy discloses active financial hardship — not as an objection but as a trust gift explaining her motivation. Randy's response is 'I gotcha. But No problem.' — procedural acknowledgment with no empathy and no bridge to how solar specifically addresses financial strain. Per coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion, the flip available here is: 'That's exactly why this matters — solar replaces the bill you're already paying.' Randy makes the safe move instead.
alternative_hypothesis: Randy may have been managing the credit-check moment carefully, not wanting to over-promise before qualification was confirmed. This is plausible but does not explain the absence of any empathetic acknowledgment.
coaching_implication: This is the call's highest-leverage missed moment. The manager should play this clip and ask: 'What would you say here?' The goal is not a scripted response — it's getting Randy to recognize that vulnerability disclosures require a human beat before procedure resumes. A suggested rephrase direction (not verbatim): acknowledge the strain specifically, then bridge to the program's no-upfront-cost structure as directly relevant to her situation.

quote: In the long term, I just got an electric car back in September, and I don't have a charger here at my house because I don't have a garage. But at some point, I would like to add that.
stewart_read: Stacy volunteers an EV and a future charging need unprompted. This is a load-expansion signal — her electricity consumption will increase materially when she adds a charger. It also signals long-term tenure and investment intent (she said she's keeping the house forever). Randy redirects correctly to the specialist for the technical question, but he does not anchor on the enthusiasm: 'You've got an EV and you're planning to charge at home — that actually makes solar even more valuable for you specifically.' The redirect is right; the energy capture is the miss.
coaching_implication: The manager should show Randy that the EV disclosure is not just a technical question to route — it's a qualification amplifier. The customer just told him her electricity load is going up and she's planning to stay in the house. That's the moment to say 'this is exactly why the design matters for you' before routing to the specialist. No numbers needed — the intent bridge is the move.

quote: Yes. My mother is on the title with me. Her name is Gail Braemer.
stewart_read: Randy captures the co-title-holder correctly per verify.title_co_owners, but never returns to Gail's inclusion in the appointment. The button-up at 13:05 books Stacy alone for tomorrow at 10AM with no mention of whether Gail needs to be present. Per protocols.spouse_decision, a co-title-holder must be on the appointment — the setter who 'wins' this without including the co-decider loses the deal. Randy's failure here is not in the capture; it's in the non-application of the protocol at booking.
alternative_hypothesis: Randy may have assessed Gail as a passive title-holder rather than an active decision-maker, given that Stacy is the one who initiated the inquiry. No evidence supports this — Randy never asked about Gail's role in the decision.
coaching_implication: The manager should walk Randy through the protocols.spouse_decision entry and ask: 'When Stacy told you her mother was on the title, what did you do with that?' The coaching is not about the capture — it's about the follow-through at button-up. The appointment is structurally vulnerable if Gail is a required signatory and isn't present.

quote: Sounds good. I'll just get you down for tomorrow at ten.
stewart_read: The button-up is missing three required elements: the 'is that fair' verbal handshake (button_up.is_that_fair_handshake), the no-driving/at-computer anchor (button_up.no_driving_at_computer), and any resolution of Gail's presence. Randy does confirm the date and time and mentions a confirmation email, which covers button_up.appointment_datetime_confirmed and button_up.text_followup_promise partially. But the handshake and computer anchor are predictive of show rate per the schema — their absence makes this booking more fragile than it needs to be.
coaching_implication: The manager should drill the full button_up checklist with Randy against this call. The 'is that fair?' handshake is a 3-second verbal commitment that the schema explicitly flags as predictive of show rate. Randy is one sentence away from a stronger hold on this appointment.

quote: Yeah. Actually, in the winter, it's more like $2.50.
stewart_read: Stacy volunteers her winter bill figure — transcript reads '$2.50' which is almost certainly a transcription artifact for $250 (per schema numeric_discipline: flag ambiguity, do not convert silently). Randy's response is 'Oh, really? For a little for a little tiny house. Yeah. My house is only 853 square feet. So Oh, yeah. I gotcha. High high bills for a small house. That's a pretty high bill.' He captures the pain signal but does not flip it per coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion. The bill is acknowledged and dropped — the universal failure pattern the schema names explicitly.
coaching_implication: Note for the manager: the '$2.50' figure in the transcript is almost certainly a transcription artifact for $250 — verify against the actual bill before using this figure in coaching. On the coaching substance: Randy heard 'high bill for a small house' and said 'yeah, that's high' and moved on. The flip available here — 'that's the bill solar replaces' — is the move the schema identifies as universally missed across the corpus. This is a teachable moment on bill_anchoring.
